title: Epiglottitis
oneLiner: Acute, rapidly progressive supraglottic inflammation causing airway obstruction; now more common in adults post-Hib vaccination, but still a paediatric airway emergency.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Complete airway obstruction** – the single finding that says “this is not routine croup/tonsillitis”: **stridor at rest** (not just on agitation) or **tripod position** (sitting forward, mouth open, chin up). If present, do NOT examine the throat; call anaesthetics/ENT immediately.
- **Septic shock** – can present with high fever, hypotension, altered GCS. Differentiate: **hypotension + stridor + mottled skin** means aggressive fluid resuscitation and antibiotics while arranging airway.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Severe, rapid-onset sore throat (hours, not days)
  - Painful swallowing (odynophagia) causing **drooling** of saliva
  - “Hot potato” voice / muffled voice (not true hoarseness)
  - Breathlessness, high fever, rigors
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Patient distressed, tripod posture, neck hyperextended
  - Stridor (inspiratory, may become biphasic)
  - In children: toxic appearance, anxious, pale
  - DO NOT use a tongue depressor – can trigger laryngospasm
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Croup** – barking cough, worse at night, steeper subglottic narrowing on X-ray; less toxic, responds to steroids
- **Bacterial tracheitis** – high fever, productive cough, “thumbprint” sign on X-ray (similar but deeper: epiglottitis has supraglottic swelling); requires bronchoscopy
- **Peritonsillar abscess** – unilateral swelling, trismus, muffled voice but no stridor; CT or ultrasound if uncertain
- **Retropharyngeal abscess** – neck stiffness, torticollis, dysphagia; CT neck with contrast shows prevertebral swelling
- **Severe tonsillitis / pharyngitis** – exudates, bilateral tonsillar enlargement, no stridor, responds to analgesics
- **Anaphylaxis** – rapid onset, urticaria, hypotension, wheeze; no fever
- **Splitter:** **Presence of stridor at rest** (not just on agitation) → epiglottitis or tracheitis; absent stridor → peritonsillar abscess/tonsillitis. **Tripod posture** is highly specific for epiglottitis.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Assess airway: Can they speak? (if aphonic → critical). If stridor at rest → do NOT send for imaging.
  - Pulse oximetry + capnography (if available)
  - Do NOT attempt oral examination or throat swab
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, blood cultures ×2 (before antibiotics if safe)
  - U&E, lactate, coagulation
  - Consider Hib serology (later for public health)
- **Imaging:**
  - **Lateral neck X-ray** – only if patient stable, sitting upright, with senior escort; “thumbprint sign” (oedematous epiglottis). Do NOT rely on normal X-ray to rule out.
  - **CT neck** – if diagnosis unclear or to exclude abscess (after airway secured)
- **Special tests:**
  - Nasolaryngoscopy by ENT (only with airway team present) – direct visualisation of cherry-red epiglottis
- **Never-miss test:** **Airway assessment (e.g. awake flexible nasendoscopy by specialist)** – omitting this can lead to sudden complete obstruction. Also **blood cultures** before antibiotics (to tailor therapy).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Airway first:** Call anaesthetics + ENT urgently. Senior airway team should be present.
  - Give high-flow oxygen via non-rebreathe mask. Have difficult airway equipment ready.
  - If stridor at rest or tripod: do not lie patient flat. Sit them up. Prepare for intubation in controlled setting (theatre/ICU). Avoid nasal cannulae if child – may agitate.
  - IV access ×2, bloods as above, start IV fluids (Hartmann’s 500 mL stat if hypotensive)
  - IV antibiotics: [check local policy] – typically ceftriaxone 2 g IV once daily (child: 50–80 mg/kg) + consider anti-staphylococcal cover (e.g. clindamycin or vancomycin) if risk factors (immunocompromised, recent surgery). Do not delay >30 min.
  - IV dexamethasone [check local policy] – may reduce oedema (e.g. 0.15 mg/kg IV/PO, max 10 mg)
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - Admit to HDU/ICU for airway monitoring. Do not discharge from ED.
  - Continue IV antibiotics for 7–10 days. Oral switch when swallowing safe and afebrile.
  - Steroids: one dose usually sufficient; taper not needed.
  - Analgesia: paracetamol + IV morphine (small doses, monitor respiratory rate). Avoid NSAIDs if coagulopathic.
  - Humidified oxygen if hypoxic.
  - ENT review daily for repeat nasendoscopy before discharge.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Follow up if underlying immunodeficiency or recurrent episodes (rare).
  - Ensure Hib vaccination status is up to date (especially children <5 yrs, unvaccinated adults).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: Hartmann’s (or 0.9% NaCl) 1–2 L/24 h, plus maintenance (e.g. 1 mL/kg/hr) – adjust for dehydration
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin if admitted >24 h and not bleeding (beware coagulopathy in sepsis)
  - Diet: NBM initially – only clear fluids if swallowing safe (speech and language therapy review)
  - Observations: NEWS2 every 1 hour (consider continuous pulse oximetry)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Ceftriaxone: 2 g IV once daily (adult); child: 50–80 mg/kg IV once daily (max 2 g). Duration: 7–10 days.
  - Add clindamycin 600 mg IV QDS (if significant Staph. aureus risk) OR vancomycin [check local policy] (loading dose 25–30 mg/kg then IVI)
  - Dexamethasone: 0.15 mg/kg IV/PO (max 10 mg) single dose
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Cefotaxime 1–2 g IV TDS (if ceftriaxone contraindicated)
  - Co-amoxiclav (if mild suspicion of airway compromise) – but less reliable for Hib
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Ceftriaxone: avoid in neonates with hyperbilirubinaemia (binds albumin), caution in penicillin allergy (low cross-reactivity; skin test if uncertain)
  - Clindamycin: pseudomembranous colitis risk
  - Vancomycin: red man syndrome – slow infusion; monitor levels
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Ceftriaxone: no dose adjustment (biliary excretion)
  - Vancomycin: adjust dose in AKI – check local protocol
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Vancomycin –** give loading dose too fast causes hypotension; always slow infusion over ≥1 h. Second: **ceftriaxone –** not compatible with IV calcium (precipitate) – flush lines between.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Complete airway obstruction / respiratory arrest
  - Post-intubation complications: laryngeal oedema, aspiration pneumonia
  - Sepsis ± septic shock
  - Epiglottic abscess
- **Chronic:**
  - Rare – vocal cord palsy (if prolonged intubation or abscess)
  - Recurrence (in immunosuppressed or those with anatomical anomalies)
sections.Prognosis: - Excellent if airway secured early; mortality <1% in children, ~1–5% in adults (mostly due to delayed airway intervention or sepsis). Full recovery expected within 7–10 days.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **When you suspect epiglottitis, summon ENT and anaesthetics immediately** – even if X-ray is pending. Do not wait for imaging.
- **Give IV antibiotics within 30 minutes** after blood cultures – ceftriaxone plus anti-staphylococcal cover in adults.
- **Always check vaccination history** for Hib – if incomplete, recommend completion after recovery.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT use a tongue depressor or ask patient to say “ah”** – can trigger laryngospasm and total obstruction.
- **Do NOT send patient to radiology alone** – must be escorted by senior doctor with airway equipment.
- **Do NOT discharge home** (even if symptoms improve) – airway can swell unpredictably. Admit to HDU/ICU.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home – E:
- **Return if:** Difficulty breathing, recurrence of stridor, voice change, drooling, or fever >38.5°C not controlled with oral antipyretics.
- **Follow-up:** ENT clinic within 1 week for repeat nasendoscopy; GP to check Hib vaccine status.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call anaesthetics/ENT immediately** for any stridor at rest, tripod posture, or if patient cannot speak.
- **Call ICU** if NEWS2 ≥6, lactate >4 mmol/L, or need for non-invasive ventilation/post-intubation care.
- **Crash team** if GCS drops, impending respiratory arrest (use difficult airway protocol).

rawMarkdown:
## Epiglottitis
**One-liner:** Acute, rapidly progressive supraglottic inflammation causing airway obstruction; now more common in adults post-Hib vaccination, but still a paediatric airway emergency.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Complete airway obstruction** – the single finding that says “this is not routine croup/tonsillitis”: **stridor at rest** (not just on agitation) or **tripod position** (sitting forward, mouth open, chin up). If present, do NOT examine the throat; call anaesthetics/ENT immediately.
- **Septic shock** – can present with high fever, hypotension, altered GCS. Differentiate: **hypotension + stridor + mottled skin** means aggressive fluid resuscitation and antibiotics while arranging airway.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Severe, rapid-onset sore throat (hours, not days)
  - Painful swallowing (odynophagia) causing **drooling** of saliva
  - “Hot potato” voice / muffled voice (not true hoarseness)
  - Breathlessness, high fever, rigors
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Patient distressed, tripod posture, neck hyperextended
  - Stridor (inspiratory, may become biphasic)
  - In children: toxic appearance, anxious, pale
  - DO NOT use a tongue depressor – can trigger laryngospasm

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Croup** – barking cough, worse at night, steeper subglottic narrowing on X-ray; less toxic, responds to steroids
- **Bacterial tracheitis** – high fever, productive cough, “thumbprint” sign on X-ray (similar but deeper: epiglottitis has supraglottic swelling); requires bronchoscopy
- **Peritonsillar abscess** – unilateral swelling, trismus, muffled voice but no stridor; CT or ultrasound if uncertain
- **Retropharyngeal abscess** – neck stiffness, torticollis, dysphagia; CT neck with contrast shows prevertebral swelling
- **Severe tonsillitis / pharyngitis** – exudates, bilateral tonsillar enlargement, no stridor, responds to analgesics
- **Anaphylaxis** – rapid onset, urticaria, hypotension, wheeze; no fever
- **Splitter:** **Presence of stridor at rest** (not just on agitation) → epiglottitis or tracheitis; absent stridor → peritonsillar abscess/tonsillitis. **Tripod posture** is highly specific for epiglottitis.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Assess airway: Can they speak? (if aphonic → critical). If stridor at rest → do NOT send for imaging.
  - Pulse oximetry + capnography (if available)
  - Do NOT attempt oral examination or throat swab
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, CRP, blood cultures ×2 (before antibiotics if safe)
  - U&E, lactate, coagulation
  - Consider Hib serology (later for public health)
- **Imaging:**
  - **Lateral neck X-ray** – only if patient stable, sitting upright, with senior escort; “thumbprint sign” (oedematous epiglottis). Do NOT rely on normal X-ray to rule out.
  - **CT neck** – if diagnosis unclear or to exclude abscess (after airway secured)
- **Special tests:**
  - Nasolaryngoscopy by ENT (only with airway team present) – direct visualisation of cherry-red epiglottis
- **Never-miss test:** **Airway assessment (e.g. awake flexible nasendoscopy by specialist)** – omitting this can lead to sudden complete obstruction. Also **blood cultures** before antibiotics (to tailor therapy).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Airway first:** Call anaesthetics + ENT urgently. Senior airway team should be present.
  - Give high-flow oxygen via non-rebreathe mask. Have difficult airway equipment ready.
  - If stridor at rest or tripod: do not lie patient flat. Sit them up. Prepare for intubation in controlled setting (theatre/ICU). Avoid nasal cannulae if child – may agitate.
  - IV access ×2, bloods as above, start IV fluids (Hartmann’s 500 mL stat if hypotensive)
  - IV antibiotics: [check local policy] – typically ceftriaxone 2 g IV once daily (child: 50–80 mg/kg) + consider anti-staphylococcal cover (e.g. clindamycin or vancomycin) if risk factors (immunocompromised, recent surgery). Do not delay >30 min.
  - IV dexamethasone [check local policy] – may reduce oedema (e.g. 0.15 mg/kg IV/PO, max 10 mg)
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - Admit to HDU/ICU for airway monitoring. Do not discharge from ED.
  - Continue IV antibiotics for 7–10 days. Oral switch when swallowing safe and afebrile.
  - Steroids: one dose usually sufficient; taper not needed.
  - Analgesia: paracetamol + IV morphine (small doses, monitor respiratory rate). Avoid NSAIDs if coagulopathic.
  - Humidified oxygen if hypoxic.
  - ENT review daily for repeat nasendoscopy before discharge.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Follow up if underlying immunodeficiency or recurrent episodes (rare).
  - Ensure Hib vaccination status is up to date (especially children <5 yrs, unvaccinated adults).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: Hartmann’s (or 0.9% NaCl) 1–2 L/24 h, plus maintenance (e.g. 1 mL/kg/hr) – adjust for dehydration
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin if admitted >24 h and not bleeding (beware coagulopathy in sepsis)
  - Diet: NBM initially – only clear fluids if swallowing safe (speech and language therapy review)
  - Observations: NEWS2 every 1 hour (consider continuous pulse oximetry)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Ceftriaxone: 2 g IV once daily (adult); child: 50–80 mg/kg IV once daily (max 2 g). Duration: 7–10 days.
  - Add clindamycin 600 mg IV QDS (if significant Staph. aureus risk) OR vancomycin [check local policy] (loading dose 25–30 mg/kg then IVI)
  - Dexamethasone: 0.15 mg/kg IV/PO (max 10 mg) single dose
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Cefotaxime 1–2 g IV TDS (if ceftriaxone contraindicated)
  - Co-amoxiclav (if mild suspicion of airway compromise) – but less reliable for Hib
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Ceftriaxone: avoid in neonates with hyperbilirubinaemia (binds albumin), caution in penicillin allergy (low cross-reactivity; skin test if uncertain)
  - Clindamycin: pseudomembranous colitis risk
  - Vancomycin: red man syndrome – slow infusion; monitor levels
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Ceftriaxone: no dose adjustment (biliary excretion)
  - Vancomycin: adjust dose in AKI – check local protocol
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Vancomycin –** give loading dose too fast causes hypotension; always slow infusion over ≥1 h. Second: **ceftriaxone –** not compatible with IV calcium (precipitate) – flush lines between.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Complete airway obstruction / respiratory arrest
  - Post-intubation complications: laryngeal oedema, aspiration pneumonia
  - Sepsis ± septic shock
  - Epiglottic abscess
- **Chronic:**
  - Rare – vocal cord palsy (if prolonged intubation or abscess)
  - Recurrence (in immunosuppressed or those with anatomical anomalies)

### Prognosis
- Excellent if airway secured early; mortality <1% in children, ~1–5% in adults (mostly due to delayed airway intervention or sepsis). Full recovery expected within 7–10 days.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **When you suspect epiglottitis, summon ENT and anaesthetics immediately** – even if X-ray is pending. Do not wait for imaging.
- **Give IV antibiotics within 30 minutes** after blood cultures – ceftriaxone plus anti-staphylococcal cover in adults.
- **Always check vaccination history** for Hib – if incomplete, recommend completion after recovery.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT use a tongue depressor or ask patient to say “ah”** – can trigger laryngospasm and total obstruction.
- **Do NOT send patient to radiology alone** – must be escorted by senior doctor with airway equipment.
- **Do NOT discharge home** (even if symptoms improve) – airway can swell unpredictably. Admit to HDU/ICU.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home – Extremely Rare, Only After Full MDT Assessment)
- **Return if:** Difficulty breathing, recurrence of stridor, voice change, drooling, or fever >38.5°C not controlled with oral antipyretics.
- **Follow-up:** ENT clinic within 1 week for repeat nasendoscopy; GP to check Hib vaccine status.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call anaesthetics/ENT immediately** for any stridor at rest, tripod posture, or if patient cannot speak.
- **Call ICU** if NEWS2 ≥6, lactate >4 mmol/L, or need for non-invasive ventilation/post-intubation care.
- **Crash team** if GCS drops, impending respiratory arrest (use difficult airway protocol).
